title: The Barnard Arnault Test: A Brutal Filter for What to Own

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
If you want to know whether an asset will hold its value over the coming decades, apply what Michael Saylor calls the Barnard Arnault test: will someone richer, more cultured, and more intelligent than you want to buy it from you in ten years? If the answer is no, you're holding something that's quietly losing value against the relentless expansion of the money supply.

The test cuts through the noise by focusing on what cannot be manufactured, replicated, or automated. A square mile of central London land purchased in 1600 would still be enormously valuable today. The buildings on it would have crumbled. The ships in the harbor would have rotted. The currency would have been debased into irrelevance. The luxury goods would be museum curiosities at best. Only the land — scarce, desirable, irreplaceable — survived four centuries of technological obsolescence and monetary debasement.

In the modern context, scarce desirable property extends beyond prime real estate. It includes cultural capital like premier sports teams and old masters paintings, intellectual property like the music rights to the Beatles catalog, mineral rights, gold, and digital capital like Bitcoin. What unites them is that affluent, intelligent people compete for ownership, and no factory or robot can produce more of them.

The test also reveals what not to own. As AI automates the drafting of legal contracts, medical opinions, and financial analyses, and as robots eventually handle physical labor, anything a machine can produce will deflate toward marginal cost. The consumer price index captures this world — packaged goods, manufactured items, streaming entertainment — but it misses the 7% annual appreciation in things robots cannot create. Understanding the difference between CPI inflation and asset inflation is essential to applying this filter correctly.

The practical implication is stark: spending £200,000 on a law degree may be a worse investment than spending £200,000 on scarce desirable property. The value of being a lawyer will be whittled down as AI delivers ten thousand lawyers for ten pounds a month. The property, if it passes the Arnault test, will compound in value as the currency debases around it.
